project_overview.project_development_objective: To support the Federal Republic of Somalia to acquire and deploy Project COVID-19 vaccines and to strengthen national immunization capacity
project_overview.implementation_period: September 28, 2021 to June 30, 2024 (Project closing date); March 31, 2025 (Program closing date)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Health and other frontline workers, elderly (over 50 years), people with co-morbidities, Internally Displaced Persons (IDPs), and ultimately the entire population of Somalia as vaccination coverage expands

primary_locations: All Federal Member States (Galmudug, Hirshabelle, Jubaland, South-West State, Puntland)
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - Somalia is explicitly identified as a fragile state with ongoing armed insurgency, Al Shabaab presence, and security risks in multiple regions. The project targets 70% of the population across all states including conflict-affected areas

key_components.description: Support deployment of COVAX vaccines and additional Project COVID-19 vaccines to cover approximately 30% of target population. Includes: transportation and supply chain logistics (US$11.0M), social mobilization efforts (US$5.0M), acquisition of additional vaccines through AVAT mechanism (US$13.0M), and healthcare waste management (US$2.0M)

Support program coordination and project management including: program management support through WHO (US$7.0M), supervision and coordination at state level (US$5.0M), and implementation support including third-party monitoring and security risk management (US$2.0M)

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Federal Ministry of Health (FMoH) with support from UN agencies (UNICEF and WHO). Project Coordination and Implementation Unit (PCIU) established within FMoH. External Assistance Fiduciary Section (EAFS) in Office of Accountant General oversees financial management
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: National Coordination Committee (NCC) for COVID-19 vaccine introduction; National Technical Working Group (NTWG); Incident Management Support Team (IMST); National Vaccine Coordination Committee for COVID-19 comprising Director Generals of Federal and FMS Ministries of Health; Project Steering Committee (PSC); sub-national committees at state level
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: Direct selection of UN agencies (UNICEF, WHO) for main implementation activities; procurement of vaccines through COVAX Facility and African Union AVAT mechanism; recruitment of Security Risk Management Company and Third Party Monitoring firm; use of World Bank's New Procurement Framework with flexibility for emergency procurement; prior review by World Bank of all procurement activities
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Results Framework with PDO indicator (proportion of population fully vaccinated by sex) and intermediate indicators (vaccine acceptance, doses administered vs allocations, social mobilization campaigns, EVM assessments, AEFI system). Monitoring every 6 months through MoH systems. Third-party monitoring firm to be contracted. Quarterly financial reports to World Bank. Annual audits by external auditor. Monitoring and supervision consulting firm to assess project compliance, UN agency performance, training effectiveness, and grievance mechanism accessibility

identified_risks.risk_description: Precarious political situation with postponed elections (to October 2021), persistent risks of political instability, renewed violence, and rising insecurity. Governance concerns at FMoH resulted in leadership changes. Political impasse makes it impossible for FGS MoH to directly support service delivery in Somaliland
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Build and leverage mitigation measures from Damal Caafimaad Project; support regular consultations and stakeholder engagement at technical level between FGS and FMS; strengthen FMoH fiduciary capacity; implement through experienced UN agencies with sound track record; maintain continuity through technical-level coordination during politically unstable periods

identified_risks.risk_description: High security risks from armed groups (Al Shabaab, militias) opposing vaccination; risks of terrorist attacks on publicized vaccination campaigns; healthcare workers targeted in past campaigns; Al Shabaab raised concerns over Astra Zeneca vaccine affecting uptake; gathering large numbers for vaccination provides easy target; reaching rural areas under armed group control is dangerous
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Develop security management framework and risk assessments by accredited security risk management company; prepare security management plans for each Federal Member State and Somaliland; use pre-existing specialist security risk management services contracted by SCRP PIU; monitor implementation of security plans; integrate security considerations into all vaccination activities

identified_risks.risk_description: Uncertainties concerning global vaccine supply chain due to steep global demand, variable manufacturer production capacity, and limited negotiating power of low- and middle-income countries. Risk of delays in vaccine availability. Feasibility of vaccinating millions at record speed in challenging country context with weak health systems
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Work through COVAX facility and African Union AVAT mechanism for vaccine sourcing; include both demand and supply side interventions in project design; use portfolio approach for vaccine acquisition adjusted during implementation; coordinate with WHO and UNICEF on vaccine acquisition strategies; support social mobilization to address demand-side constraints

identified_risks.risk_description: Modest institutional and management capacity at FMoH; weak government fiscal situation limiting resource mobilization; heavy dependence on external financing; VIRAT/VRAFT assessment highlighted key gaps in vaccination readiness; limited experience with World Bank operations; capacity constraints in planning, budgeting, contracting, and oversight
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Contract key activities to WHO and UNICEF while building FMoH oversight capacity; leverage government experience contracting UN agencies under SCRP CERC; use recently approved Damal Caafimaad Project to bolster institutional capacity at FMoH and FMS; recruit additional specialists to PCIU (social development, environment, GBV, security); provide World Bank hands-on training and capacity building; support through SCRP PIU in first phase

identified_risks.risk_description: Financial management challenges at FMoH affecting project implementation; recent governance incidents highlighted need for substantial support in FM and procurement; limited financial management competencies and internal controls; reliance on consultants; lack of regulatory framework for key PFM aspects; weak banking and financial sector with AML/ATF risks; limited number of competent procurement staff; fraud and corruption risks; collusion among bidders
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Use External Assistance Fiduciary Sections (EAFS) for financial management oversight; contract UN agencies to reduce fiduciary risks; recruit qualified Procurement and Financial Management Specialists to PCIU; use IPSAS Cash Basis accounting; implement prior review by World Bank of all procurement; conduct periodic training on World Bank procedures; use World Bank's STEP system for procurement tracking; engage external audit firm; implement strong internal controls and documentation requirements; establish Project Fixed Assets Register and Contracts Registers

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial macroeconomic risks due to limited government capacity to respond to external shocks and modest fiscal space. Economy dependent on agriculture vulnerable to climate shocks. COVID-19 pandemic has worsened economic outlook and disrupted livelihoods in country where 70% live in poverty
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Ongoing support from development partners including IMF to expand revenue mobilization; World Bank financing to support vaccination program; coordinate with other development partners on resource mobilization

identified_risks.risk_description: While various strategies and plans (NDVP, National Vaccine Coverage and Purchase Plan, Priority Groups) have been elaborated, they remain to be finetuned, fleshed out, and finalized. Implementation capacity to operationalize plans is limited
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: World Bank coordination with WHO, UNICEF, GAVI to assist authorities to make plans fully operational; support through technical assistance and capacity building; regular monitoring and adaptive management

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial environmental risks including: occupational health and safety risks for healthcare workers; community exposure to infection; healthcare waste management risks due to capacity constraints; vaccine storage and transportation risks; cold chain logistics environmental impacts; increased medical waste from vaccine containers, masks, gloves; risks from fossil fuels for off-grid power; potential contamination of soil and groundwater; limited resources for waste management in low-income Somalia
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Prepare Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) before effectiveness; develop Infection Prevention and Control - Waste Management Plan (IPC-WMP); prepare area-specific Environmental and Social Management Plans (ESMPs); implement waste management protocols; provide training on healthcare waste management in flood-prone areas; procure climate-friendly cold chain equipment (solar direct drive refrigerators, low GWP refrigerators); develop contingency plans for vaccine distribution during climate emergencies; monitor climate-related activities

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial social risks including: elite capture and exclusion of vulnerable groups (IDPs, nomads, minorities, people with disabilities) from vaccine benefits; clan-based health seeking behavior disadvantaging minorities; vaccine hesitancy due to misinformation and rumors; gender barriers to women accessing vaccines (limited mobility, low health literacy, restrictive norms); risks of sexual exploitation and abuse, sexual harassment, and GBV; labor risks including poor terms and conditions; lack of functional grievance redress; contextual challenges in conducting effective community consultations; lack of trust in service providers
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Identify clear criteria and equitable sequencing for vaccine allocation aligned with WHO SAGE framework; deploy independent groups for third-party monitoring; engage local CSOs for citizen engagement and oversight; establish accessible grievance mechanisms with multiple intake points; conduct sensitization and capacity building on SEAH risks; ensure regular community consultations on SEAH risks and reporting measures; develop SEAH Prevention and Response Plan; integrate SEAH requirements into all ESMPs; prepare Labor Management Plan outlining fair treatment and non-discrimination; ensure adequate terms and conditions of employment; tailor communication materials for women and vulnerable groups; support women-friendly vaccination platforms; track vaccine acceptance by women; leverage female health workers as role models; engage religious leaders and community leaders; conduct social mobilization to address vaccine hesitancy; establish hotlines for information and rumor tracking

identified_risks.risk_description: Vaccine hesitancy is major risk due to perceptions pandemic is under control, stigma, rumors, and misinformation. Access barriers for different stakeholders due to geographic and cultural impediments. Women face multiple barriers including limited mobility, low health literacy, restrictive gender norms, and risks of violence traveling to vaccination centers. IDPs and nomadic populations face accessibility challenges. Concerns about vaccine effectiveness and side effects. Trust issues with service providers
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Implement multi-pronged demand generation and community engagement strategy; mobilize faith leaders, local authorities, social media; conduct rumor tracking and social listening; establish two-way conversations at community level; train health and community workers and media; establish hotlines for information; develop tailored communication strategies for different groups; leverage trusted sources (religious leaders, nomadic elders, influential figures); use multiple platforms (radio, social media, women's groups); conduct operational research to understand vaccine uptake obstacles and enablers; support vaccine champion/ambassador program; deploy community mobilizers; conduct briefings with journalists; create social media materials and audio-visual content; ensure women-friendly platforms and tailored communication; support outreach to IDPs and nomadic populations; address misinformation through accurate information campaigns

triggered_safeguard_policies: Environmental and Social Standard 1 (Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts)
Environmental and Social Standard 2 (Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure)
Environmental and Social Standard 3 (Labor and Working Conditions)
Environmental and Social Standard 4 (Community Health and Safety)
Environmental and Social Standard 5 (Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Project-level grievance redress mechanism to be established within 2 months after Effective Date. Multiple intake points for feedback and grievance reporting at all levels. Separate and confidential mechanisms for receiving complaints of sexual exploitation and abuse, sexual harassment, and other forms of gender-based violence. Links to mechanisms at point of service. Established protocols for survivor-centered responses. Links to wider beneficiary feedback mechanisms for service standards and beneficiary satisfaction. Accessible to project-affected people. World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS) also available for complaints
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Gender inequality is significant societal problem in Somalia with one of highest gender inequality indices globally. Women face barriers to accessing health services, employment, and have limited autonomy. Initial vaccination phase showed women represented only 25% of vaccinated. Project will support: tailored communication materials targeting women; women-friendly vaccination platforms; training of health workers to address needs of female clients; tracking and monitoring of vaccine acceptance by women; leveraging voices of Somali women to promote vaccine acceptance; support for women-led community platforms; special emphasis on reaching women through outreach activities; addressing barriers including limited mobility, low health literacy, and risks of violence
vulnerable_groups: People living in rural and remote areas

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed for fragile and conflict-affected context. Incorporates conflict sensitivity through: security risk assessments and management plans for each FMS and Somaliland; engagement of specialist security risk management company; security considerations integrated into all vaccination activities; coordination with armed forces under control of MoH/UN agencies; security protocols for staff safety during campaigns; security arrangements for vaccine storage and transportation; targeting of vaccination campaigns to minimize security risks; use of multiple delivery channels (facility-based and outreach) to reach populations in different security contexts; engagement of community leaders and religious leaders to build trust and reduce opposition; social mobilization to counter misinformation from armed groups; contingency planning for vaccine distribution in insecure areas; third-party monitoring to track security incidents; coordination with UN One COVAX Taskforce for security considerations
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Somalia is among poorest countries with 70% living in poverty. Multiple fragility factors: ongoing armed insurgency and Al Shabaab presence; frequent natural disasters (droughts, floods, locust infestations); humanitarian crises with millions internally displaced; political crises and weak institutional capacities; limited fiscal capacity and domestic resource mobilization; weak health system with low service coverage; limited human resources (0.92 health workers per 1000 people vs WHO minimum 2.3); health facilities inadequate and inequitably distributed; high burden of disease; limited government role in service delivery with NGOs providing most services; weak regulatory capacity; governance problems; limited capacity of Federal Member States; political tensions between FGS and some FMS and Somaliland; recent HIPC Initiative Decision Point (March 2020) reopening access to concessional financing; ongoing institutional and political reforms but progress fragile
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: High security risks explicitly identified and addressed: Al Shabaab and other armed groups oppose vaccination and have targeted healthcare workers; armed groups control some regions; risks of terrorist attacks on publicized vaccination campaigns; healthcare workers face assault risks; vaccine shortages can lead to violence; security personnel conduct requires monitoring; security risk assessments and management plans required for each state; security management framework to be developed; security risk management company to conduct assessments and prepare plans; security considerations in vaccine storage, transportation, and distribution; security protocols for staff safety; coordination of security activities under control of MoH/UN agencies; monitoring of security incidents through third-party monitoring; use of pre-existing security risk management services from SCRP PIU
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Internally Displaced Persons (IDPs) are significant vulnerable population. Estimated 2.6 million IDPs in Somalia. Project specifically targets IDPs as priority group for vaccination (included in Phase 3 - all other adults over 18 years). Special efforts planned to ensure equitable access to IDPs including: identification of IDPs through standardized process; tailored strategies for reaching IDPs; community health workers and community leaders to identify and register difficult-to-reach groups including IDPs; outreach activities to camps for IDPs; mobile clinics to reach IDPs; social mobilization campaigns tailored to IDPs; monitoring of vaccine acceptance among IDPs; grievance mechanisms accessible to IDPs. Climate-induced displacement projected to increase as impacts of climate change intensify. Project includes climate adaptation measures to support vaccination continuity during climate shocks that may cause displacement
